body.question_1: Reflecting on the full academic year, how would you rate your overall educational experience at this university?
body.answer_1: My overall academic year experience in the Computer Science program at the School of Information Technology has unfortunately been inadequate and has fallen considerably short of the expectations I had when I enrolled. Persistent issues with teaching quality, administrative responsiveness, infrastructure reliability, and student support have collectively impacted my ability to learn and thrive in this environment. I feel that despite making this feedback heard through multiple channels, meaningful change has been slow and the institution's response to student concerns has lacked urgency and accountability. I sincerely hope the upcoming academic year brings substantive improvements in all the areas I have identified, not only for my own benefit but for all students who will follow in future cohorts.

body.question_2: How effectively do you believe this year's coursework has prepared you for your future career or further academic studies?
body.answer_2: Career preparation support from the Career Center at the School of Information Technology has been substandard and has left many students — including myself — feeling ill-equipped for the competitive graduate employment market. Career workshops are infrequent, poorly promoted, and often scheduled at times that conflict with lecture commitments, reducing student participation. The internship database is outdated and contains many positions that are no longer active, and support for students during internship placements is minimal to nonexistent. I point out the university to substantially reinvest in the Career Center — expanding its employer network, modernizing its tools, and increasing the frequency and quality of career development programming across all academic programs and year levels.

body.question_3: How would you assess the progression of your academic skills and knowledge throughout this academic year?
body.answer_3: The Computer Science curriculum at the School of Information Technology appears to lag behind current industry standards in several significant areas that I believe require urgent review and revision. Many courses continue to teach outdated tools and methodologies that are rarely used in professional settings today, leaving students underprepared for actual workplace demands. The curriculum also lacks sufficient elective depth and specialization pathways, limiting students' ability to develop expertise in niche areas of growing importance. I urge the Academic Affairs Office and school leadership to conduct a thorough curriculum audit in close consultation with industry professionals and alumni to ensure course content remains relevant, rigorous, and career-oriented.

body.question_4: How satisfied are you with the extracurricular activities, student clubs, and personal development opportunities available?
body.answer_4: The extracurricular opportunities available at the School of Information Technology, coordinated by the Student Affairs Office, are exceptional and represent a genuinely important complement to the formal academic curriculum. A wide and diverse range of student clubs, cultural societies, sports teams, and volunteer programs are actively supported and well-resourced by the university, enabling students to develop leadership skills, build social connections, and pursue interests outside their academic programs. Events are well-promoted, regularly scheduled, and inclusive in their design, welcoming students from all backgrounds and programs to participate equally. I value the institution's recognition that holistic student development extends beyond classroom performance and into every dimension of campus life.

body.question_5: How well has the university's Career Center prepared you for internship opportunities and graduate employment?
body.answer_5: The career preparation support provided by the Career Center at the School of Information Technology has been impressive and has made a tangible difference to my professional readiness and employment prospects as I approach graduation. Resume writing workshops, mock interviews, and industry networking events have been well-organized, highly practical, and attended by credible industry professionals who provided genuinely actionable feedback. The internship matching program is effective, with clear communication about available placements and strong follow-up support for students during their internship periods. I value the Career Center's proactive approach and its strong relationships with employers, which have opened real opportunities for students across all programs.

body.question_6: How would you rate the university's financial services, including tuition fee management and scholarship support?
body.answer_6: The financial services provided by the Finance Department at the School of Information Technology have been outstanding this semester, and I genuinely recognize the clarity, transparency, and efficiency with which they are managed. Fee statements are issued promptly and accurately, with clear breakdowns of tuition, facility charges, and any applicable scholarship deductions that make it easy to verify and plan payments. Payment options are flexible and well-communicated, including installment plans that meaningfully reduce financial pressure on students and families. Scholarship disbursements are processed on time and confirmed through official notifications, removing uncertainty around critical financial support. The finance office staff are professional, patient, and genuinely helpful when navigating complex financial queries.

body.question_7: How would you evaluate the IT infrastructure and digital services provided by the university over the course of the year?
body.answer_7: The IT infrastructure at the School of Information Technology, maintained by the IT Support Department, performs at a decent level that meets basic student needs but falls short of what is required to fully support a modern, technology-dependent academic environment. Wi-Fi connectivity is generally stable in primary teaching spaces but becomes inconsistent during peak usage hours and in peripheral campus locations. Software availability in computer labs is adequate for core coursework but limited for students requiring specialized applications for advanced projects. Expanding bandwidth capacity, updating hardware on a regular replacement cycle, and improving helpdesk responsiveness would meaningfully elevate the digital learning experience for the entire student community.

body.question_8: How satisfied are you with the library resources — both physical and digital — that were available throughout the academic year?
body.answer_8: Learning resources for my Computer Science program at the School of Information Technology are decent, with some courses providing excellent, well-curated materials and others offering minimal guidance beyond the core required textbook. The library's digital collection is reasonable in scope but has some notable gaps in specialized resources relevant to advanced topics in our field. The LMS functions adequately as a distribution platform, though its use varies significantly between lecturers, creating an inconsistent experience across modules. A more standardized approach to resource provision, with minimum content requirements per course, would considerably improve the consistency and quality of the learning environment.

body.question_9: How effectively did the university communicate important information, deadlines, and updates throughout the academic year?
body.answer_9: The academic administrative processes at the School of Information Technology, overseen by the Academic Affairs Office, have been a source of considerable frustration for many students this semester. Course registration was plagued by system errors and conflicting timetables that took weeks to resolve, causing students to miss early enrollment advantages. Grade dispute processes are opaque and slow, with students waiting over a month without updates or acknowledgment of their submissions. Communication from administrative offices is reactive rather than proactive, and staff sometimes provide inconsistent information depending on who is contacted. I point out a comprehensive review of administrative workflows, with investment in both system improvements and staff training as a matter of urgency.

body.question_10: What are your key recommendations for improving the overall student experience in the coming academic year?
body.answer_10: I have very few concerns to raise about my overall experience in the Computer Science program at the School of Information Technology, and I genuinely believe this institution provides an superb educational environment that prepares students well for their future careers. If I were to recommend any improvement, I would suggest further expanding industry partnership opportunities — additional mentorship programs, sponsored capstone projects, and guest lecture series from senior professionals would enrich an already strong academic offering. Increasing the frequency of student feedback collection and demonstrating visible action on that feedback would further strengthen trust between students and the institution. Overall, I am proud to be a student here and would highly recommend this university to future applicants without hesitation.
